title: NOC 92100 – Power engineers and power systems operators | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 92100 professionals are the specialized technical force responsible for the operation and maintenance of the reactors, turbines, and boilers that power Canada's national infrastructure. As a TEER 2 occupation, this group ensures the continuous delivery of electricity, heat, and refrigeration to hospitals, industrial plants, and commercial centers. In 2026, as Canada aggressively expands its nuclear and renewable energy capacity, Power Engineers are classified as "Critical Talent" for the country's net-zero transition. For immigration, this role is a premier candidate for Express Entry Category-Based Selection (STEM/Trades) and the OINP Employer Job Offer stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Level2Major Group92 – Processing, manufacturing and utilities supervisorsSub-Major Group921 – Utilities equipment operators and controllersMinor Group9210 – Utilities equipment operators and controllersNOC Code92100

common_job_titles: Control Room Operator - Electrical Power Systems
duties: Operations: Operate automated or computerized control systems, reactors, boilers, turbines, and generators to provide power, heat, and refrigeration.
System Control: Start up and shut down plant equipment, control switching operations, and coordinate transmission loads and frequency.
Monitoring: Inspect computer terminals, switches, valves, and gauges to detect leaks or malfunctions and ensure maximum efficiency.
Maintenance: Troubleshoot and perform minor repairs to prevent system failure; clean and lubricate turbines, pumps, and compressors.
Safety & Records: Analyze instrument readings, maintain daily logs of safety activities, and write reports on plant operation and non-compliance.
Load Management: (Power Systems Operators) Coordinate generating station power loads to meet distribution demands during outages or repairs.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is mandatory.
Power Engineers: Require a college training program in power engineering and a provincial/territorial certificate of competency according to class (Classes 1 through 5).
Power Systems Operators: Require completion of a 3-to-5-year apprenticeship program or over 3 years of trade experience plus college courses.
Nuclear Operators: Control room operators at nuclear plants require specific licensing from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC).
Moving from one class to another (e.g., from 4th Class to 3rd Class) requires documented work hours and passing mandatory exams.
salary_prospects: Note: 1st Class Power Engineers in nuclear or heavy industrial facilities often earn annual salaries exceeding $150,000 inclusive of overtime and shift premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the utilities sector will face a significant "Leadership Gap" as nearly 20% of the current workforce reaches retirement age by 2028. The expansion of small modular reactors (SMRs) and hydrogen plants across Canada ensures a long-term growth trajectory for this occupation through the end of the decade.
labour_market_demand: The demand for Power Engineers is Very Good, with a persistent labor shortage reported in 2026 across major provinces. According to Job Bank data, the "Green Energy" shift and the refurbishment of nuclear stations in Ontario and New Brunswick have created an urgent need for licensed operators. In Western Canada, the demand is driven by high-pressure steam requirements in the oil sands and heavy manufacturing sectors.
High-Tech Energy Sector: Work with some of the world's most advanced nuclear (CANDU), hydroelectric, and biomass systems.
Top-Tier Benefits: Over 98.9% of workers in this NOC receive comprehensive non-wage benefits, including employer-sponsored pensions and full medical insurance.
Recession-Proof Career: As an essential service, Power Engineering offers unparalleled job security and stable, year-round employment.
PR Strategic Pathway: This role is explicitly targeted in Category-Based Express Entry draws for 2026, offering lower CRS cut-offs for experienced applicants.
pathways: Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted under both STEM and Trades categories in 2026.
OINP Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): Frequent draws for Power Engineers to support Ontario’s industrial and healthcare sectors.
Saskatchewan SINP (Occupations In-Demand): High priority for Power Engineers supporting the province's mining and energy sectors.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure targeting skilled workers already in Canada contributing to essential utilities.
